name: Recruitment Pathway Sensitivity and Diagnostic Confidence Thresholds
consequences: Immediate: Stricter pre-admission screening (requiring prior PSG or specialist polysomnography confirmation) increases time and cost per enrolled participant but reduces false admissions and sensor-capture failures. Systemic: Relaxing the screening to accept structured interview plus bed-partner collateral would boost recruitment velocity by an estimated 25–35%, but introduce 15–20% of participants who lack objective parasomnia confirmation and yield zero captured events, increasing unproductive bed-nights and administrative overhead without improving phenotyping power. Strategic: This trade-off determines whether the unit meets the 50–70 enrollment target efficiently or exhausts contingency budget on unproductive admissions before reaching statistical power for Aim 2 longitudinal analysis.
options: Require objective parasomnia confirmation via prior clinical polysomnography or a screening PSG before admission, accepting longer recruitment timelines and referral dependencies but maximizing event-capture yield and publication-quality participant characterization.

Accept participants with high-confidence clinical interview plus collateral history from bed partner or household member, supplemented by a structured symptom inventory (frequency, injury history, sleep-stage timing) scored to a standardized threshold, balancing recruitment speed against a modest increase in screen-failure risk.

Implement a two-tier admission pathway: fast-track high-confidence referrals with prior PSG immediately, while recruiting interview-only candidates into a parallel 4-week pilot cohort with mandatory event-detection gates—retain only those producing at least one adjudicated event, re-screen others, and use aggregate yield data to auto-adjust inclusion thresholds quarterly.
review: Controls diagnostic certainty at entry vs. recruitment momentum and cost. Weakness: The options do not address how to manage the bed-partner availability bias in regions with high single-occupancy housing, which may systematically exclude certain participant demographics and skew phenotypic representativeness.

name: Ambient Sensor Density and False-Alarm Burden on Annotation
consequences: Immediate: Deploying all ambient sensors (contact-free mattress, dual infrared cameras, directional microphone array, environmental sensors) on every night captures maximal context but generates 500–800 discrete event flags per participant per week. Systemic: Automated pre-filtering reduces manual review by an estimated 40–50%, but imperfect algorithmic specificity creates 200–300 ambiguous cases requiring human adjudication per participant per study—overwhelming the night technician annotation capacity and delaying scoring turnaround, directly reducing the per-participant event detection rate. Strategic: Over-instrumentation risks drowning the team in noisy data, while under-instrumentation sacrifices the multimodal context needed to train semi-automated triage tools, forcing a choice between rich algorithm inputs and operational feasibility.
options: Deploy full ambient sensing every night with a machine-learning pre-filter trained on a 10-participant pilot cohort to flag candidate events, iteratively refining the filter thresholds over the first 6 months to achieve <30% false-positive flag rate before scaling annotation workload.

Operate scheduled ambient sensing on the three enhanced-night PSG occasions (night 1, mid-stay, final night) plus technician-triggered escalation nights, reserving full ambient data capture only after an event is confirmed clinically, reducing nightly annotation volume to ~50–100 flags per participant while capturing rich context around confirmed episodes.

Implement a hybrid sensor model: continuous low-resolution ambient data (mattress and single infrared camera) every night as a gross movement filter, supplemented by full sensor activation (dual cameras, microphone array, environmental monitors) only on nights when the mattress sensor exceeds a custom movement-intensity threshold, balancing cost-of-acquisition against cost-of-annotation and tuning sensitivity quarterly based on missed-event review.
review: Controls data richness and algorithm training input vs. annotation labor and daily operational throughput. Weakness: The options neglect the interaction between seasonal variation in sleep architecture (winter consolidation vs. summer fragmentation) and ambient noise profiles, which may systematically bias false-alarm rates across the 3-year recruitment window.

name: Staffing Rotation Model and Night Coverage Risk
consequences: Immediate: A dedicated 3-technician night-shift rotation (each working 2–3 nights per week) ensures consistent presence but locks in high fixed labor costs (~€450K of the €2M personnel budget). Systemic: Shifting to a 2-technician model with on-call backup would save ~€200K annually (~7–10% of total personnel budget) but increases false-alarm response delays from <2 min to 5–10 min, raising injury liability and missing brief high-frequency parasomnia episodes whose physiological signature (e.g., leg EMG bursts during confusional arousal) spans 3–5 seconds. Strategic: Understaffing directly erodes data quality for Aim 2 phenotyping and increases regulatory risk if a participant incident occurs during a delayed response, while overstaffing early may be unsustainable if year-two participant volume disappoints.
options: Maintain the planned 3-technician night-shift rotation with staggered 24-month contracts, hiring the third technician conditionally in month 6 contingent on enrollment reaching 8 concurrent participants, preserving safety margins and annotation capacity while deferring fixed costs until demand is proven.

Deploy a 2-technician core rotation supplemented by a standing agreement with the University Hospital Bonn sleep lab for on-call backup availability (15–30 min response window), negotiated as a cost-sharing inter-departmental arrangement, reducing base staffing costs but introducing inter-institutional coordination dependencies.

Establish a tiered staffing ladder: 2 full technicians for baseline operations plus a pool of 3–4 per-diem research assistants trained in basic event response and data export, called in on nights with confirmed high-yield participants (those with ≥2 events in prior weeks), scaling labor costs to actual event density rather than fixed participant census.
review: Controls safety coverage and annotation timeliness vs. fixed labor costs and operational flexibility. Weakness: The options do not address retention risk or burnout thresholds for night-shift workers in a small residential facility over a 3-year period, which may force unplanned departures and training-pipeline strain not captured in per-shift cost assumptions.

name: Facility Expansion Pacing and Bed Utilization Targets
consequences: Immediate: Maintaining the planned 8-suite start with deferred expansion to 12 preserves €400K–500K in year-one renovation capital and keeps per-suite operational overhead manageable during proof-of-concept. Systemic: If year-one enrollment reaches 18–20 concurrent participants, the 8-suite cap will achieve ~85% occupancy and generate a waiting list, compressing the participant accrual timeline and accelerating the 50–70 enrollment target by an estimated 4–6 months, but constraining the phenotypic diversity if recruitment outpaces the facility's ability to admit. Strategic: Premature expansion to 12 suites commits capital before validating that recruitment yield and data quality are sustainable, risking stranded capacity if enrollment stalls; delayed expansion forfeits opportunity cost if demand is genuine and other research groups establish competing facilities.
options: Expand to 10 suites after month 9 if concurrent enrollment exceeds 15 participants and inter-rater reliability scores >85% on the first 100 adjudicated events, scaling incrementally to match validated operational performance and proven recruitment momentum.

Defer all expansion beyond 8 suites until month 18, using the delay to publish early methods-validation and phenotyping findings, secure year-two grant milestones, and develop the semi-automated triage tool to full benchmarking; add 2–3 suites only if month-18 external review confirms both recruitment sustainability and algorithm readiness.

Open a second mini-unit of 4–5 suites in a separate Bonn location (non-University Hospital-affiliated, e.g., private clinic or residential hotel partnership) in year two, positioned as a replication site to test recruitment scalability and equipment portability while keeping the main unit at 8 suites, trading expansion speed for geographic and operational diversification.
review: Controls capital deployment timing and risk concentration vs. recruitment velocity and market responsiveness. Weakness: The options assume stable recruitment yield across years one and two but do not address how seasonal referral patterns (e.g., summer vacations, winter depression peaks) or competing recruitment from neighboring sleep centers might compress or extend the enrollment window unpredictably.

name: Semi-Automated Triage Algorithm Governance and Publication Timing
consequences: Immediate: Developing the event-triage model in-house over months 12–24 (Aim 3) keeps intellectual property and methodology within the academic team's control but requires data scientist time competing with real-time annotation and data curation. Systemic: Delaying algorithm benchmarking until year three (after dual-scoring is complete for ≥50 participants) would reduce false-confidence claims but postpone the first publication on computational methods by 12–18 months, slowing team visibility and delaying the submitted phase-two grant proposal required at 36 months. Strategic: Publishing the triage tool too early (on <20 events) overstates generalizability; publishing too late misses the competitive timing window for follow-on funding and invites skepticism from reviewers asking why computational benchmarking was deferred until after the main phenotyping effort.
options: Publish a methods paper on the semi-automated triage pipeline (sensitivity, specificity, and inter-rater agreement metrics) using data from the first 30–40 adjudicated events by month 18, framed explicitly as an interim algorithmic prototype, with a follow-up validation paper planned for month 30 using the full cohort dataset to demonstrate generalization and robustness.

Withhold algorithm publication until month 24–28, after dual-scoring is complete for ≥50 participants, presenting a single comprehensive benchmarking paper with confidence intervals, subgroup performance analyses (by parasomnia type, participant age, event duration), and external validation recommendations, trading publication velocity for methodological rigor and reduced revision burden.

Adopt a rolling-publication approach where algorithm updates are documented quarterly in a dedicated open preprint series (bioRxiv) with accompanying code releases on GitHub under a restricted-access license until the final peer-reviewed paper, enabling community feedback and early visibility while maintaining reproducibility standards and deferring proprietary claims until the flagship peer-reviewed manuscript appears.
review: Controls publication momentum and competitive advantage vs. methodological rigor and algorithmic maturity. Weakness: The options do not specify governance of downstream use if pharmaceutical or diagnostic device partners express interest in licensing the triage algorithm before the team has published, creating potential conflicts between premature commercialization and academic publication norms.

name: Matched Control Cohort Strategy and Baseline Comparator Design
consequences: Immediate: Enrolling a parallel matched control group (10–15 healthy adults without parasomnias, undergoing identical 2-week residential monitoring) adds €120K–180K in direct costs and recruiting overhead but provides ground-truth normal-behavior baseline data for the semi-automated triage tools. Systemic: Controls reduce false-positive sensor flags by an estimated 30–40%, enabling triage algorithms to distinguish parasomnia-specific patterns (burst activity, EEG signatures, positional clustering) from routine nocturnal movements and arousals; omitting controls forces the algorithm to be trained on case-only data with no negative-class examples, inflating apparent specificity by 15–25% and risking overfitting. Strategic: Controls are essential for Aim 3 algorithm credibility but compete with participant-compensation budgets; a smaller or shorter control cohort (4–6 participants, 1 week each) sacrifices statistical power for cost savings, while a robust control design (15+ participants, 2 weeks) strengthens peer-review credibility but reduces the budget margin for participant retention and staff contingency.
options: Recruit a full matched control cohort (12–15 healthy adults age and gender-matched to the parasomnia group, with identical 2-week residential stays on the same enhanced-night PSG and ambient-sensor protocol), treating controls as a scientific investment in algorithm generalizability and publishing a comparative methods paper describing sensor-data characteristics in normal sleep.

Implement a hybrid control strategy: enroll 6–8 controls for the year-one pilot cohort to prototype the baseline phenotype, publish a limited comparison at month 18, then conditionally enroll an additional 6–8 controls in year two only if the parasomnia cohort reaches 40+ events and algorithm development progresses on schedule, deferring expanded control recruitment until proof-of-concept.

Outsource control-group data collection to a collaborating sleep center (e.g., another university sleep lab with an existing healthy-sleep registry) under a data-sharing agreement, providing those collaborators with algorithm-benchmarking access to the triage pipeline in exchange for their control dataset, reducing primary-site recruitment burden but introducing external data-harmonization complexity.
review: Controls internal validation rigor and algorithm specificity vs. recruitment scope and budget allocation. Weakness: The options overlook how medication use in controls (antihistamines, sedating antidepressants, sleep aids) may differ from the parasomnia group, potentially confounding the sensor-data baselines and necessitating detailed control stratification that is not addressed in any option.

name: Data Sharing Strategy and Repository Deposition Timing
consequences: Immediate: Depositing de-identified physiological and sensor data (PSG waveforms, actigraphy, non-video environmental sensors) into a public repository (OpenNeuro, PhysioNet, or Sleep-EDF successor) within 3 months of study completion enhances scientific reproducibility and positions Bonn as a resource for the international sleep research community. Systemic: Early deposition (month 30–32) accelerates secondary-use discovery and citations but exposes the Bonn team to rapid competing analyses from external groups, potentially reducing the 3-year team's publication window by 6–12 months; delaying deposition to month 36 (coinciding with final publications) preserves publication priority but risks journal editor requests for pre-publication data release, creating scheduling tensions. Strategic: Public data sharing is mandatory for DFG-funded research and enhances funding-agency credibility, but video privacy constraints and GDPR compliance require careful de-identification protocols that consume data-management time and may necessitate a dedicated legal review not budgeted in current personnel planning.
options: Deposit physiological and environmental sensor data (excluding all video) to OpenNeuro under a BIDS-compatible structure by month 33, with a 12-month publication embargo requested (allowing the Bonn team first-use publication rights) and requiring secondary users to complete a minimal data-use agreement acknowledging GDPR restrictions on re-identification attempts.

Establish a controlled-access repository partnership with University of Bonn's medical informatics department, hosting de-identified data on a secure institutional server with case-by-case access granted to approved researchers upon submission of a research plan and ethics approval, preserving tighter privacy governance while still enabling external validation of methods and algorithms without full open deposition.

Implement a phased deposition schedule: release sensor-only data (mattress, actigraphy, environmental, excluding EEG) immediately after publication of the methods paper (month 18–20) as a pilot dataset, followed by full PSG and triage algorithm outputs 6 months after the final phenotyping publication, allowing the team to establish publication presence before data flooding and enabling later-joining collaborators to contribute to secondary analyses.
review: Controls scientific transparency and community resource contribution vs. publication-priority protection and privacy-governance complexity. Weakness: The options assume that de-identification of physiological time-series (PSG waveforms) is trivial, but re-identification via external EEG databases and individual-level artifact patterns is feasible in principle, and none of the options specify a formal re-identification risk assessment or anonymization-failure contingency plan.

name: Event Capture Yield Target and Screening Stringency Trade-Off
consequences: Immediate: Stricter pre-admission screening (requiring documented prior PSG evidence or bed-partner corroboration) filters participants to higher episode-frequency cases. Systemic: This raises mean captured events per participant from an estimated 60% to ~85% within the 2–8 week window, reducing zero-event admissions from 20% to ~8%, but increases screening rejection rate by 35–45%, potentially slowing enrollment velocity and extending recruitment timeline by 2–4 months. Strategic: Tighter screening accelerates Aim 2 phenotyping completeness but trades off recruitment flexibility and diversity, forcing a choice between prioritizing event abundance or participant heterogeneity.
options: Retain current 2–episode-per-month self-report criterion with lenient adjudication, accepting ~20% unproductive admissions and triggering the protocol review gate if frequency exceeds that threshold.
Require prior polysomnography or high-confidence bed-partner corroboration plus self-report, raising the bar moderately and projecting ~10–12% unproductive admissions.

Implement a pre-admission video-recorded structured sleep history and witnessed episode questionnaire scored against a validated parasomnia screening scale (e.g., PASES or equivalent), admitting only those scoring above the 75th percentile and projecting <5% unproductive admissions.
review: Controls recruitment speed vs. event-yield certainty. Weakness: The options do not account for the clinical and ethical burden of denying or deferring admission to patients with genuine parasomnia complaints who fall below the stringency threshold.

name: Real-Time Technician Annotation Burden and Dual-Rater Scoring Latency
consequences: Immediate: Night technicians currently annotate events in real time during 8-suite overnight shifts, creating cognitive load during safety-critical hours. Systemic: If annotation complexity or event frequency rises beyond current assumptions, technician accuracy may decline by 10–15% and response times to flagged incidents may exceed safety targets; adding a second overnight reviewer would increase personnel costs by ~€180K annually but reduce per-event scoring latency by 3–5 days. Strategic: This decision shapes staffing structure (9 vs. 11 FTE), directly impacts budget feasibility and timeline to publication, and determines whether real-time event escalation (enhanced-night PSG capture) is reliable or becomes a bottleneck.
options: Maintain single-technician annotation on night shift with asynchronous dual-rater review during business hours, accepting 4–7 day latency between event flag and confirmed diagnosis and requiring technician retraining on prioritization during high-activity nights.

Deploy a rotating second night technician for 3 nights per week (peak-capture coverage), sharing annotation duty, reducing latency to 2–3 days for 60% of events, and incurring ~€95K annual incremental cost.

Introduce a remote on-call sleep technologist from the university sleep lab who reviews real-time video feeds and sensor streams asynchronously via secure VPN, providing expert triage within 2 hours and escalating high-confidence events for same-night PSG capture, costing ~€120K annually plus infrastructure hardening.
review: Controls annotation work distribution vs. diagnostic latency and event-escalation responsiveness. Weakness: None of the options address the risk that technician fatigue or high noise-event burden from ambient sensors might degrade the quality of real-time triage regardless of staffing model.

name: Facility Occupancy Ramp and Fixture Maturation Sequencing
consequences: Immediate: Launching with 8 suites rather than all 12 allows 3–6 months of operational debugging (safety protocols, technician workflow, sensor reliability, false-alarm calibration) with lower financial risk. Systemic: Staying at 8 suites through month 18 reduces participant throughput to ~30–35 per year and delays reaching the 50–70 target enrollment until year 3, but each participant receives higher-quality attention; scaling to 12 suites by month 14 would accelerate enrollment to 45–50 per year, supporting year 2 milestone completion, at the risk of operational strain if staffing or protocol maturity lags. Strategic: This lever determines whether the unit achieves its enrollment and phenotyping goals on time and within budget, and whether the external failure trigger (25 participants or 40 events by month 18) is met comfortably or triggering an uncomfortable external review.
options: Maintain 8-suite operation through month 18 (end of year 1 pilot), freeze recruitment at 25–30 participants in that window, and implement a formal facility audit at month 16 before committing to 4-suite expansion in year 2.

Scale incrementally to 10 suites by month 10 (adding one unit per quarter after months 4 and 7), hiring technician staffing on the same cadence, targeting 40–45 admissions by month 18 and reaching 12 suites by month 20.

Accelerate to full 12-suite capacity by month 8, hiring all remaining technicians and supervisory staff by month 6, and projecting 55–60 participant admissions by month 18 if recruitment channels are opened fully and support functions scale proportionally.
review: Controls operational maturation time vs. enrollment velocity and cost acceleration. Weakness: The options do not differentiate based on recruitment channel capacity or regional sleep referral network saturation, which may be the binding constraint rather than suite availability.

name: Matched Control Recruitment Strategy and Baseline Characterization Depth
consequences: Immediate: A matched control group provides clean denominators for event-triage algorithm benchmarking and false-positive sensor rates under normal sleep. Systemic: Recruiting and retaining 20–25 matched controls (age, sex, sleep duration, absence of parasomnia history) to the same 2–8 week in-unit protocol requires ~18–24 months of parallel recruitment and incurs cost parity with patient enrollment; alternatively, pooling existing normative actigraphy or PSG data from the university sleep lab avoids recruitment overhead but sacrifices in-unit sensor equivalence and introduces hidden confounds, potentially inflating false-positive rates by 10–20%. Strategic: Control-group fidelity directly impacts the credibility of Aim 3 (event-triage benchmarking) for publication and future clinical validation; weak controls weaken the entire algorithm narrative and reduce impact.
options: Recruit 25 matched controls through community volunteer channels and employee networks, admitting them to the same residential protocol for 5–7 nights with identical sensor suites but explicit monitoring for absence of parasomnia and zero clinical events, completing enrollment by month 24.

Leverage archived, de-identified polysomnography and actigraphy data from the University Hospital Bonn sleep lab for historical controls matched on age, sex, and BMI, supplementing with 8–10 in-unit reference nights from healthy department staff, reducing new recruitment burden by 70% but accepting methodological heterogeneity.

Partner with a neighboring sleep research program (e.g., in Cologne or Düsseldorf) to cross-recruit matched controls and split in-unit monitoring costs, requiring formal data-sharing agreements and adding 4–6 months to coordination timelines but achieving true external validation.
review: Controls recruitment symmetry vs. methodological rigor and cost containment. Weakness: The options underestimate the attrition risk for healthy controls undertaking 5–7 nights in a residential facility; drop-out rates for non-patient volunteers historically exceed 15–20%, potentially invalidating sample matching.

name: Data Deposition Scope and Pre-Publication Embargo Window
consequences: Immediate: Early (month 18–20) deposition of de-identified physiological and sensor data to a public repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, XNAT) enables rapid community reuse and strengthens publication impact. Systemic: Depositing raw multi-modal sensor streams plus AASM-scored event labels 3–6 months after publication allows third-party algorithm development and reproducibility checks, accelerating secondary science; a 12-month embargo window delays community access but protects first-author advantage on algorithm development and provides breathing room for methods corrections, reducing retraction risk by ~30% but weakening immediate open-science optics. Strategic: This decision shapes the unit's reputation trajectory (leadership in open-science vs. competitive advantage in algorithm ownership) and influences future collaboration and funding partnerships; early deposition signals confidence and attracts algorithm developers but forfeits publication strategy control.
options: Deposit de-identified physiological and raw sensor data (EEG, actigraphy, infrared frame counts, audio spectra, environmental data) with AASM event annotations immediately upon first publication (month 20–22), without embargo, establishing rapid community resource and inviting immediate third-party replication.

Implement a 12-month rolling embargo tied to first publication date, allowing the core team protected priority for algorithm manuscript completion and initial validation work before opening the dataset, with clear repository language signaling embargo end-date and encouraging future collaborative proposals.

Restrict initial deposition to processed summary-level features and de-identified event descriptors (e.g., episode duration, peak muscle tone, arousal type) rather than raw sensor streams, deferring full raw-data release to year 3, minimizing privacy risk and maximizing first-author control over algorithm development.
review: Controls open-access advocacy vs. competitive publication strategy and privacy assurance. Weakness: The options do not specify whether video-adjacent metadata (e.g., time-stamped incident location, inferred room occupancy) will be included in 'de-identified' data, creating potential re-identification risk even if facial imagery is excluded.

name: Autonomous Sensor Escalation Sensitivity and Technician Override Authority
consequences: Immediate: The tiered acquisition model currently relies on human technician judgment to flag events for same-night enhanced PSG escalation; deploying rule-based sensor thresholds (e.g., sudden bed acceleration + breath hold + arousal spike = escalate) automates this triage. Systemic: High-sensitivity escalation (flagging ~40% of nights) maximizes event capture but floods the PSG unit, requiring 2–3 additional PSG runs per week and increasing technician overtime by 15–20% and consumables cost by ~€12K/year; conservative thresholds (flagging ~15% of nights) preserve efficiency but miss ~25–30% of borderline episodes, lowering overall event yield and potentially violating the Aim 2 completeness goal. Strategic: This lever directly impacts the feasibility of Aim 3 (algorithm benchmarking) — the semi-automated triage tool must be trained and tested against human decisions, so automating the initial human triage step risks circularity and corrupts the independent gold-standard comparison.
options: Preserve technician discretion as the sole escalation trigger, requiring each night technician to maintain a running mental model of sensor activity and decide independently whether to call enhanced PSG, with quarterly calibration meetings to ensure consistency but no formal algorithmic rules.

Implement decision-support rules (e.g., accelerometry + 2+ arousal bursts within 10 minutes → escalate) that highlight candidate nights for the technician but preserve final override authority, with a monthly audit showing 5–10% of technician rejections of rule-suggested escalations to assess for systematic biases.

Deploy an unsupervised anomaly detector (e.g., isolation forest on multi-modal sensor space) trained on the first 50 admissions that ranks nights by suspicion score; technicians review top-N candidates (N = 8–10 per week) and retain veto power, but resource-constrained decision-making is removed.
review: Controls human judgment fidelity vs. systematic consistency and workload rationalization. Weakness: None of the options account for the correlation between technician fatigue (e.g., after an active night) and subsequent night accuracy, potentially biasing escalation decisions in unpredictable directions.

name: Pharmaceutical Partnership Engagement Timing and Independence Boundaries
consequences: Immediate: A pharma partner (optional upside, not required for feasibility) brings €300–500K year 2–3 funding, covering personnel expansion or extended participant compensation and accelerating recruitment. Systemic: If a partner is secured by month 6, they may request outcome reporting on specific drug-adjacent phenotypes (e.g., frequency response to medication clusters, genetic biomarker discovery), potentially narrowing scientific scope by 15–20% and delaying non-pharma-priority publications by 3–6 months; conversely, declining partnership preserves publication autonomy but may force budget cuts or enrollment compression if DFG and institutional funding encounter delays. Strategic: Early partnership decision shapes the unit's identity (independent academic facility vs. industry-linked research site) and influences recruitment messaging, participant expectations regarding data use, and publication timing across all three aims.
options: Pursue a pharmaceutical partnership immediately (by month 4), negotiating a data use agreement that defines pharma-funded phenotyping workstreams (e.g., response heterogeneity to exemplar medications) alongside core aims, with the explicit 30-day pre-publication review clause and full transparency to participants and IRB.

Defer partnership discussions until month 12, after Aim 1 and early Aim 2 data are complete and independent publication strategy is locked in, then invite pharma engagement for Aim 3 algorithm work or expansion-phase studies, retaining year 1–2 autonomy.

Decline pharmaceutical partnership entirely and seek supplementary funding through the DFG European Research Council (ERC) Starting Grant scheme or international collaborative grants, preserving unconditional academic independence but accepting lower total budget and potentially slower scaling.
review: Controls funding diversity and growth velocity vs. intellectual independence and publication prioritization. Weakness: The options do not address the reputational risk of post-hoc partnership announcement; early transparency is critical to maintain participant trust and regulatory compliance, but mid-course partnership pivots create perception of hidden conflicts.

name: Episode Frequency Threshold and Admission Stringency Calibration
consequences: Immediate: Stricter pre-admission event frequency documentation (e.g., requiring 4+ episodes per month rather than the stated 2+) filters lower-frequency cases and increases probability of in-residence capture. Systemic: Higher admission threshold reduces expected event yield by approximately 15–25%, but increases captured events per bed-month by ~30% (since admitted cohort has higher intrinsic frequency); this accelerates Aim 2 phenotyping but narrows generalizability to milder presentations. Strategic: A stricter gate prioritizes publication timelines and Aim 2 completion over inclusivity, trading depth-of-analysis against breadth-of-phenotype representation and reducing the facility's clinical utility for lower-burden parasomnias.
options: Require minimum 4 episodes per month documented over 3 months pre-admission, with bed-partner or actigraphy corroboration, admitting only high-frequency, well-characterized cases.

Retain the stated 2+ per month criterion but implement a 2-week screening actigraphy baseline; admit participants showing ≥2 events in that window, adjusting stay duration downward for higher-frequency cases.

Accept the 2+ per month self-report without actigraphy screening, extending the ceiling on unproductive admissions from 20% to 35% and increasing maximum stay to 12 weeks to maximize recruitment volume and inclusivity.
review: Controls recruitment stringency vs. event-capture velocity. Weakness: The options do not account for the cost-per-adjudicated-event metric, which may dominate operational viability if cheaper screening upstream (e.g., prospective actigraphy) is not funded.

name: Escalation Sensor Array Specificity and Technician Decision Latency
consequences: Immediate: Tuning ambient microphone sensitivity and mattress-sensor threshold (e.g., peak acceleration ≥0.5g for movement detection) determines how many possible parasomnia events trigger full PSG escalation versus routine annotation alone. Systemic: Lowering thresholds captures ~40% more potential events but increases technician decision instances by 50–70%, risking alert fatigue and delayed response to genuine high-priority events; raising thresholds reduces false escalations by ~60% but may miss ~20% of genuine episodes occurring with subtle signatures. Strategic: This lever controls the feedback loop between sensor sensitivity and technician workload, directly influencing annotation quality and safety response times; tighter specificity improves staff sustainability but risks undercapture of real events, while loose thresholds maximize event yield but degrade technician reliability and may exceed the 3-technician roster's real-time capacity.
options: Deploy a learned escalation model trained on a 4-week pilot dataset, ranking events by morphologic and contextual probability (e.g., mattress acceleration + microphone burst + EEG state + time of night) and automatically queuing only top-20% candidates for technician review, with override authority to manually flag lower-ranked events.

Set fixed thresholds (e.g., mattress ≥0.7g AND microphone dB ≥65) requiring simultaneous sensor signatures to trigger escalation, minimizing single-sensor false positives and keeping technician decision load constant at approximately 3–5 events per night.

Implement a night-shift-specific sensitivity curve that relaxes thresholds during hours 1–3 (highest parasomnia density) and tightens during hours 6–8, with a weekly technician audit of missed vs. flagged events to dynamically recalibrate sensitivity based on operational load.
review: Controls sensor specificity vs. real-time detection responsiveness. Weakness: None of the options incorporate technician fatigue modeling or include a mechanism for detecting threshold drift over time, risking slow degradation of escalation quality as staff become desensitized to alerts.

name: Dual-Rater Scoring Pipeline Sequencing and Annotation Concurrency
consequences: Immediate: Deciding whether independent raters score videos sequentially (rater A completes all nights for a participant, then rater B repeats) versus in parallel (both raters score independently on overlapping schedules, with discrepancy resolution) affects annotation timeline and inter-rater concordance detection speed. Systemic: Parallel scoring reduces time-to-publication for Aim 2 analyses by ~4–6 weeks (raters work concurrently rather than serially), but requires 80–100% more scoring person-hours in year one and may overwhelm the clinical psychologist's capacity if external scorers are not hired; sequential scoring preserves single-rater workload but delays disagreement detection by 6–8 weeks, risking late discovery of scoring protocol drift. Strategic: This lever couples annotation workforce planning to publication velocity and methodological robustness; parallel scoring prioritizes speed and early-stage reproducibility validation but demands budget expansion, while sequential scoring preserves budget constraint but risks losing operational flexibility if post-hoc retraining becomes necessary.
options: Score all nights in parallel with two independent raters from month 3 onward, budget €15K for external sleep medicine physician scorer contracting, and implement weekly kappa concordance audits to detect and correct scoring protocol drift in real time.

Assign rater A to score nights 1–8 weeks sequentially per participant, then rater B rescores the full dataset only after cohort enrollment closes, accepting delayed discrepancy detection but preserving single-rater continuity and clinical psychologist capacity.

Implement a hybrid model where raters score high-priority nights (first, mid-stay, final) in parallel and escalation nights sequentially, reducing external hiring to ~€7K and maintaining Aim 2 timeline while capping year-one scoring labor at the clinical psychologist's available hours.
review: Controls annotation concurrency vs. workforce cost and protocol coherence. Weakness: The options fail to specify how scoring protocol updates (e.g., clarifications on confusional arousal morphology) are communicated if raters are working asynchronously, creating risk of systematic disagreement mid-study.

name: Matched Control Cohort Depth and Baseline Sensor Calibration Investment
consequences: Immediate: Deciding the scope and recruitment timeline for the matched control group (age, sex, BMI-matched to parasomnias cases) determines the reference dataset for false-positive sensor behavior and baseline arousal patterns. Systemic: Recruiting 15–20 matched controls (50% of patient cohort) and admitting them for identical 2–4 week stays provides high-quality per-person baseline data, enabling sensor false-positive rates to be estimated at ~5–10% specificity precision, but diverts ~€120–150K from patient recruitment and extends overall enrollment timeline by 3–4 months. Smaller control pools (5–8 participants) reduce costs by ~€40K but yield broader confidence intervals on sensor specificity (±15–20%), weakening statistical power for downstream event-triage algorithm benchmarking. Strategic: This lever determines the evidentiary threshold for the semi-automated triage tools in Aim 3; robust controls enable high-confidence sensitivity/specificity claims suitable for future clinical deployment or regulatory pathways, while lean controls preserve recruitment momentum for patients but risk publishing weak algorithmic claims, affecting grant-funding prospects for phase-two work.
options: Enroll 18–20 age- and sex-matched healthy controls for 2-week stays in year one, conduct sensor baseline measurement during week 1 (undisturbed sleep) and sensor-escalation protocol testing during week 2, and reserve this cohort exclusively for Aim 3 algorithm benchmarking with primary publication focus on false-positive characterization.

Enroll 8–10 controls during months 6–12 of year one, admit for a single 7-night baseline period collecting low-burden sensor data only (no PSG escalation), and include control nights as a secondary validation set for the triage algorithm with lower statistical power but minimal timeline disruption.

Partner with an external sleep laboratory in Cologne (40 km away) to contribute 12 pre-existing healthy-control actigraphy datasets with retrospective sleep diaries, supplementing them with 4–6 in-resident controls, reducing direct enrollment burden by 50% but introducing potential heterogeneity in sensor acquisition protocols and baseline measurement context.
review: Controls control-cohort investment scope vs. algorithm evidentiary rigor. Weakness: External partnership options (third option) introduce unmeasured confounds in sensor deployment and environment (home vs. laboratory), potentially invalidating specificity estimates if sensor behavior is environment-dependent.

name: Real-Time Annotation Completeness and Post-Hoc Scoring Window Trade-Off
consequences: Immediate: The night technician can annotate events in-real-time during their shift (immediate flagging of parasomnia events and pseudoseizure-like episodes for safety escalation) or defer detailed annotation to a morning review window (technician focuses solely on safety monitoring, with annotation deferred 8–12 hours). Systemic: Real-time annotation ensures acute safety response and produces same-night event metadata for escalation PSG deployment, but splits technician cognitive load (simultaneous monitoring, annotation, participant care) and may reduce annotation accuracy by ~15–20% due to fatigue and divided attention; deferred annotation preserves technician focus on safety and surveillance but delays escalation PSG by one day, risking event recurrence capture latency and introducing a 1-night gap in high-resolution characterization. Strategic: This lever couples safety responsiveness to annotation precision; real-time annotation maximizes safety and event-pair clustering but risks technician error and burnout, while deferred annotation protects data quality but introduces temporal gaps that may compromise Aim 2 phenotyping completeness and event-frequency estimation if participants are discharged before recurrent events occur.
options: Require real-time annotation by the night technician during all active monitoring; provide a second part-time technician (0.5 FTE) for participant care and safety patrol to decouple monitoring from annotation, increasing staffing cost by ~€40K/year but preserving acute escalation capability.

Implement morning annotation review only, with the night technician documenting safety events and flagged sensor alerts in a brief incident log; escalation PSG deployment occurs only if a morning annotation confirms a likely parasomnia event, accepting a 1-night delay for standard escalation but preserving technician cognitive capacity.

Adopt time-zone-shifted asynchronous annotation where the morning clinical psychologist reviews overnight videos and sensor streams during business hours and deploys escalation PSG decisions by midday, transferring annotation burden from night staff to day staff and introducing a 6–12 hour decision latency acceptable for non-urgent events.
review: Controls real-time safety responsiveness vs. annotation cognitive load. Weakness: None of the options quantify the cost or feasibility of hiring a part-time safety technician; if unavailable, the first option becomes infeasible, forcing a choice between the other two and narrowing strategic flexibility.

name: Facility Expansion Greenlight Criteria and Phased Utilization Ramp
consequences: Immediate: The plan defers suites 9–12 expansion until pilot data (months 0–8) validates safety, data quality, and staffing viability. Conditional expansion approval requires explicit metrics: continuous safe operation for ≥6 months, ≥25 enrolled participants, false-alarm rate <30% of nights, and annotation throughput ≥95% complete within 48 hours. Systemic: Meeting all thresholds by month 8 triggers a €350K capital investment (physical buildout, additional sensors, network expansion) and adds 0.5 FTE technician (~€45K/year), increasing operational capacity to 12 suites and raising annual participant throughput from ~35 to ~50+, accelerating Aim 2 phenotyping by ~6 months and reducing per-event cost by ~12%; if metrics are missed, deferral to month 12 or facility right-sizing to 6 active suites preserves budget and extends Aim 2 timeline by 9+ months. Strategic: This lever determines scaling velocity and long-term financial sustainability; aggressive expansion accelerates publication momentum and competitive positioning but risks operational overstretching if staffing or data quality declines, while conservative deferral preserves operational margins and staff morale but may miss funding windows or lose participant momentum.
options: Trigger expansion immediately upon achieving 3/4 conditional thresholds by month 8 (e.g., safe ops + enrollment + throughput), deferring false-alarm rate validation to post-expansion, allowing flexible scaling if initial conditions warrant it.

Enforce all four metrics strictly; approve expansion only if continuous safe operation ≥6 months AND ≥25 participants AND false-alarm <30% AND annotation completion ≥95%, with automatic deferral to month 12 if any single metric misses, preventing reactive over-commitment.

Decouple physical expansion from staffing: add 2 suites (9–10) in month 8 without hiring additional technicians, introducing a temporary 2-night/week reduced-occupancy constraint (suites 9–10 dark on alternate nights), deferring full 12-suite staffing to year two only if month-12 enrollment exceeds 35 participants.
review: Controls scaling velocity vs. operational stability and staffing margin. Weakness: The options do not define fallback actions if expansion metrics are missed but participant demand is high, risking frustration with referral sources if applicants must be turned away.

name: Pharmaceutical Partnership Collaboration Scope and Data-Access Boundaries
consequences: Immediate: If pharmaceutical industry funding and expertise is leveraged (optional upside, not a feasibility requirement), a partnership agreement specifies data-access rights, publication timeline, and co-authorship scope. Systemic: A 'comment-only' 30-day pre-publication window (as stated in the plan) preserves academic independence and enables timely Aim 2 publications with no delays; a deeper partnership offering pharma co-authorship or dedicated analysis lanes (e.g., outcomes restricted to a specific drug subgroup) trades publication velocity for ~€400–600K additional funding in year 2, reducing university budget pressure but introducing ~3–4 week publication delays for consensus on messaging and potentially constraining generalizability claims to avoid commercial optics. Strategic: This lever couples funding diversification to publication autonomy and scope; light partnerships maximize academic agility and Aim 3 algorithm openness, while deeper partnerships provide financial cushion and industry credibility but risk public perception of bias and may delay phase-two grant competitiveness if results appear narrowly scoped to a single therapeutic class.
options: Pursue a strictly academic-led model with university and DFG funding only, declining pharmaceutical partnership entirely; preserve full publication independence and rapid timeline but accept higher operational risk if year-2 budget pressures emerge.

Secure a pharma partnership with a 30-day pre-publication comment window on all publications, non-exclusive data-access rights (pharma may conduct internal analyses), and naming rights only if pharma contributes ≥€300K in year 2, maintaining academic authority while diversifying revenue.

Establish a deeper pharma co-development agreement where pharma funds a dedicated analysis lane (e.g., pharmacogenetic subgroup analysis or outcomes in a specific diagnostic category) in exchange for co-authorship on targeted publications, adding €500K in year 2 but introducing potential publication delays and scope constraints on algorithmic claims.
review: Controls academic independence vs. financial security and industry partnership depth. Weakness: The options do not address conflict-of-interest management or the governance process for resolving disagreements between academic and industry teams on manuscript messaging, risking ethical challenges or publication delays if tension arises.
